title: Action and Use of Circuit Components — Hard

questions.question: A potential divider contains a thermistor and a $100\text{ }Omega$ fixed resistor connected to a $6\text{ V}$ cell. The thermistor resistance is $500\text{ }Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and $50\text{ }Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$. What is the voltmeter reading across the thermistor at $100^\circ\text{C}$?
questions.explanation:
At $100^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{therm} = 50\text{ }Omega$ and $R_{fixed} = 100\text{ }Omega$. Total $R = 150\text{ }Omega$. $V_{out} = (50 / 150) \times 6 = 1/3 \times 6 = 2.0\text{ V}$. (Note: The Practice 17.4 q1 in the source asks for the reading across the fixed resistor, which would be $4.0\text{ V}$).

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potential divider has a variable resistor $R_1$ (range $0\text{--}4\text{ }Omega$) and a fixed resistor $R_2 = 6\text{ }Omega$ connected to a $12\text{ V}$ source. What are the minimum and maximum possible values of $V_{out}$ measured across $R_2$?
questions.explanation:
Max $V_{out}$ is when $R_1 = 0$: $V = (6/6) \times 12 = 12\text{ V}$. Min $V_{out}$ is when $R_1 = 4$: $V = [6 / (4+6)] \times 12 = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2\text{ V}$. This matches logic from Review Q9.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student wants to design a circuit that turns on a lamp when the temperature falls below a certain level. Which arrangement of a thermistor and a relay would be most appropriate?
options: Thermistor in a potential divider to activate a relay when its resistance is high
Thermistor in a potential divider to activate a relay when its resistance is low
Thermistor connected in series with the lamp and a d.c. supply
Thermistor used as a fuse to break the circuit when it gets too cold
questions.explanation:
When it gets cold, a thermistor's resistance increases. A potential divider can be used to provide enough voltage to a relay to switch on a secondary high-power lamp circuit once that resistance threshold is reached.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potential divider uses a $9\text{ V}$ source, a $24\text{ }Omega$ fixed resistor, and a variable resistor ($0\text{--}12\text{ }Omega$). To obtain a $V_{out}$ that can vary from $0\text{ V}$ to $3\text{ V}$, how should the variable resistor be connected?
options: Measure $V_{out}$ across the variable resistor which is in series with the fixed resistor
Measure $V_{out}$ across the fixed resistor which is in series with the variable resistor
Connect both resistors in parallel with the voltmeter
The desired range is impossible with these components
questions.explanation:
If $V_{out}$ is across the variable resistor $R_{var}$: at $0\text{ }Omega, V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$. At $12\text{ }Omega, V_{out} = [12 / (12 + 24)] \times 9 = (12/36) \times 9 = 1/3 \times 9 = 3\text{ V}$. This confirms the design from Review Section B Q5.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A $12\text{ V}$ supply is connected to a $500\text{ k}Omega$ resistor and an LDR in series. In the dark, the LDR resistance is $1\text{ M}Omega$. What is the potential difference across the LDR in the dark?
questions.explanation:
$1\text{ M}Omega = 1000\text{ k}Omega$. Using the formula: $V_{LDR} = [1000 / (500 + 1000)] \times 12 = (1000 / 1500) \times 12 = 2/3 \times 12 = 8.0\text{ V}$. This matches the logic for Review Q10.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
